title: Basement wall water ingress observed at construction joint
observation: Active water ingress is visible at the basement wall joint, with staining extending below the suspect construction joint and moisture tracking toward the floor slab edge. No immediate structural distress is evident, but the leakage suggests a compromised waterproofing detail or joint seal failure.
recommended_action: Mark and photograph the full extent of seepage and note elevation/chainage references.; Inspect the joint detailing, membrane continuity, and any penetrations or tie-hole repairs.; Check adjacent drainage, sump operation, and external water management conditions.; Implement temporary water control measures and isolate any affected electrical or finish-sensitive areas.; Raise a corrective action for waterproofing specialist review and retesting after repair.
escalation: Escalate immediately if leakage increases, reaches active electrical equipment, or shows signs of wall movement, cracking, or soil washout.
image_caption: Damp staining and active seepage visible along a horizontal joint on a basement retaining wall, with minor pooling at the floor-wall junction.
visual_clues: Dark damp streaks on cast wall surface
root_cause_hypothesis: Likely discontinuity in waterproofing membrane, inadequate joint preparation, or failed waterstop/sealant at a construction joint combined with hydrostatic pressure.
offline_cache_reason: Retain as an offline seed because basement waterproofing defects are frequent, image-recognizable, and useful for baseline retrieval without internet dependency.

title: Concrete column honeycombing detected after form removal
observation: A reinforced concrete column shows honeycombing in the lower lift area following formwork removal. The defect is localized but extends over enough surface area to indicate poor consolidation or leakage during casting, requiring assessment of depth and impact on cover.
recommended_action: Sound the affected area and carry out a depth assessment by engineer or competent inspector.; Verify reinforcement cover and determine whether structural repair is required.; Review pour sequence, vibration records, and formwork tightness for the affected lift.; Protect the area from impact and restrict loading until disposition is confirmed.; Prepare a repair method statement if patching, epoxy injection, or breakout and reinstatement is needed.
escalation: Escalate to structural engineer immediately if defect depth approaches reinforcement, cover is compromised, or multiple columns exhibit similar honeycombing.
image_caption: Rough, voided concrete surface with exposed aggregate and localized cavities at the lower portion of a reinforced concrete column.
root_cause_hypothesis: Probable inadequate vibration, congested reinforcement impeding flow, grout loss through formwork joints, or improper concrete placement technique.
offline_cache_reason: Retain as an offline seed because concrete quality defects are core CV-RAG examples with stable visual characteristics and common inspection language.

title: Rebar congestion visible at beam-column joint
observation: The beam-column joint appears heavily congested, with limited access for concrete placement and vibration. The reinforcement arrangement may still be within design intent, but the visual buildability risk is elevated due to reduced clear spacing and potential cover noncompliance.
recommended_action: Compare installed reinforcement against approved shop drawings and bar bending schedules.; Confirm minimum clear spacing, lap locations, and cover compliance before pour.; Coordinate with structural designer on any permissible bar adjustments or coupler alternatives.; Plan concrete placement with smaller aggregate, controlled lift heights, and dedicated vibration access.; Document the joint with pre-pour photos and sign-off before closing forms.
escalation: Escalate if required clear spacing cannot be achieved, if cover blocks cannot be installed correctly, or if the pour is proceeding without engineer approval.
image_caption: Dense reinforcement cluster at a beam-column joint with closely spaced bars, tight tie wire, and limited visible concrete flow path.
visual_clues: Reduced clear space for concrete flow
Difficulty aligning cover blocks or spacers
root_cause_hypothesis: Likely a combination of design-dense reinforcement at a highly loaded joint and insufficient constructability coordination between structural detailing and placement method.
offline_cache_reason: Retain as an offline seed because reinforcement congestion is a repeatable pre-pour condition that benefits from cached visual exemplars and checklist guidance.

title: MEP duct coordination clash at ceiling void
observation: The ceiling coordination area shows a clear clash between ductwork and other services, reducing available clearance for ceilings and maintenance access. The condition appears to be a coordination issue rather than an immediate safety hazard, but it may drive rework if unresolved.
recommended_action: Verify current coordinated shop drawing against installed service positions.; Identify the conflicting elements and quantify the clearance shortfall.; Hold the affected trade install and route the clash to coordination review.; Confirm access requirements for dampers, valves, and future maintenance before finalizing layout.; Issue revised setting-out instructions and record any approved deviations.
escalation: Escalate to project coordination lead if ceiling closure is imminent, access to critical maintenance components is blocked, or multiple trade clashes are present.
image_caption: A large rectangular duct intersects the planned ceiling service zone, with nearby pipework and cable tray creating a tight coordination conflict.
visual_clues: Hangers and supports appear tightly packed
Potential conflict with lighting or access panels
root_cause_hypothesis: Likely incomplete coordination of MEP trades, late design revisions, or insufficient clash detection before installation.
offline_cache_reason: Retain as an offline seed because MEP clashes are common in visual QA workflows and useful for training cross-trade reasoning in a disconnected environment.

title: Unsafe open edge adjacent to scaffold access point
observation: An open edge is present immediately adjacent to scaffold access, creating a high-risk fall exposure for workers transitioning between the scaffold and the slab. The hazard is uncontrolled and requires immediate protection or access restriction.
recommended_action: Stop access to the affected route until edge protection is installed or alternative safe access is provided.; Install compliant guardrails, toe boards, and controlled access measures at the perimeter.; Inspect scaffold tie-ins, landing condition, and opening protections for related defects.; Brief all nearby crews on the exclusion and safe access route.; Record the hazard in the permit or daily safety log and verify closure before restart.
escalation: Escalate immediately to site management and safety leadership; work in the area must remain stopped until the fall hazard is fully controlled.
image_caption: Unprotected slab edge with no visible guardrail at the landing area next to scaffold access, exposing a fall hazard.
visual_clues: Open edge without top rail or mid-rail
Scaffold access ladder or landing nearby
No visible toe board at edge
root_cause_hypothesis: Likely removal or omission of edge protection during sequencing, inadequate supervision, or incomplete scaffold-to-structure interface controls.
offline_cache_reason: Retain as an offline seed because open-edge fall hazards are high-value safety examples and visually obvious for CV-RAG detection even without live connectivity.

title: Hairline crack observed near lift core wall opening
observation: A narrow crack is visible near the lift core wall, close to an opening or re-entrant corner. At the moment it appears to be a hairline surface condition, but its location warrants monitoring because core walls can attract stress concentrations and construction-stage movement.
recommended_action: Measure and mark crack length, width, and orientation using a calibrated gauge.; Check whether the crack is live by monitoring for seasonal or load-related movement.; Review recent pours, stripping sequence, and temporary loading around the core wall.; Inspect for associated moisture ingress, spalling, or adjacent cracking patterns.; Escalate for structural review if the crack widens, propagates, or appears through-thickness.
escalation: Escalate to structural engineer if crack width exceeds project tolerance, shows displacement, or is accompanied by leakage or progressive propagation.
image_caption: Thin vertical crack line visible near the lift core wall corner, with no obvious displacement or spalling at the surface.
visual_clues: Localized around opening or stress concentration
No active leakage or spalling apparent
root_cause_hypothesis: Possible shrinkage crack, restraint from adjacent pours, temporary load transfer effects, or stress concentration at an opening corner.
offline_cache_reason: Retain as an offline seed because crack monitoring is a common inspection scenario and supports visual differentiation between benign and potentially structural defects.

title: Water ingress approaching temporary electrical riser
observation: Water ingress is occurring close to a temporary electrical riser, creating an immediate risk of electrical shock, short circuit, and service interruption. The condition is time-sensitive because the seepage path may reach live distribution components if not isolated.
recommended_action: Isolate the affected temporary electrical circuit and verify de-energization by a competent person.; Keep personnel away from the wet area and establish a controlled exclusion zone.; Identify the water source and apply temporary diversion, containment, or pumping as needed.; Inspect the riser for ingress, damaged insulation, and elevated termination points.; Restore power only after the area is dry, the source is controlled, and electrical integrity is confirmed.
escalation: Escalate immediately to electrical supervisor and site safety if live equipment is threatened, if any arc/odor/heat is observed, or if isolation cannot be verified.
image_caption: Moisture staining and seepage are visible on the floor near a temporary electrical riser, with damp routing nearby.
visual_clues: Water pooled close to temporary cabling
Damp streaks on adjacent wall or floor
Potential contact between water path and electrical installation
root_cause_hypothesis: Likely failed waterproofing, rainwater intrusion, or uncontrolled surface drainage compounded by insufficient protection and routing of temporary electrical infrastructure.
offline_cache_reason: Retain as an online-enriched incident because it benefits from live-context examples of temporary power exposure, but it also serves as a high-priority safety retrieval record when connectivity is available.

title: Falling object exclusion zone breached beneath overhead work
category: Dropped Object Risk / Site Access Control
observation: Personnel have entered an exclusion zone established for overhead work, exposing them to falling object hazards. The area control is ineffective or not being enforced, increasing the likelihood of impact injuries from tools, materials, or debris.
recommended_action: Stop overhead work until the drop zone is fully re-established and enforced.; Remove unnecessary materials, tools, and loose items from elevated workfaces.; Rebrief all affected workers on exclusion limits and access permissions.; Assign a spotter or marshal to prevent unauthorized entry into the hazard area.; Review whether overhead work requires additional netting, tool lanyards, or rigid barricades.
escalation: Escalate to the area supervisor and HSE lead if repeated breaches occur, if overhead lifting is active, or if hard barricading is not feasible.
image_caption: Workers are present inside a taped-off area beneath elevated work, with materials and tools staged above.
root_cause_hypothesis: Likely poor access control, weak supervision, unclear signage, or failure to maintain separation during simultaneous operations.
offline_cache_reason: Retain as an online-enriched record because falling object control often needs updated situational context and is valuable for live incident pattern matching.

title: Concrete spalling at slab edge after formwork striking
observation: Spalling is visible at the slab edge after formwork striking, indicating localized damage during stripping or early-age concrete weakness. The defect may be superficial, but edge integrity and reinforcement cover need confirmation before accepting the pour.
recommended_action: Suspend further aggressive stripping or edge cleaning until the affected area is assessed.; Check whether reinforcement cover is exposed and whether repair depth is limited to surface concrete.; Review striking method, curing duration, and concrete strength test results for the pour.; Document the defect extent and protect the edge from impact or additional loading.; Complete approved repair and curing measures before reopening the slab edge for use.
escalation: Escalate to the engineer if reinforcement is exposed, if the spall extends beyond a localized patch, or if similar damage is found at multiple edges.
image_caption: Chunks of concrete missing from the slab edge with rough fractured surfaces following formwork removal.
root_cause_hypothesis: Likely premature striking, excessive prying force, insufficient concrete curing strength, or weak edge detailing at the formwork interface.
offline_cache_reason: Retain as an online-enriched record because post-pour damage patterns benefit from fresh examples and can correlate with operational sequencing data.

title: Temporary works prop shows visible deformation
observation: A temporary works prop is visibly deformed, indicating possible overload, instability, or foundation settlement. This is a critical condition because failure of temporary support could trigger partial collapse or sudden load redistribution.
recommended_action: Immediately clear personnel from the supported area and prohibit loading on the temporary arrangement.; Inspect all related props, bracing, and bearing conditions for signs of instability or settlement.; Verify the approved temporary works design against actual loading and geometry.; Install additional supports only under the direction of the temporary works coordinator or engineer.; Record the defect and do not reinstate use until the system is recertified safe.
escalation: Escalate immediately to the temporary works coordinator, construction manager, and safety lead; treat as a potential collapse precursor.
image_caption: A support prop beneath a temporary load-bearing arrangement is visibly bowed, with uneven bearing at the head and base plates.
root_cause_hypothesis: Possible overload from premature loading, inadequate bracing, incorrect prop capacity selection, or base settlement on an insufficient bearing surface.
offline_cache_reason: Retain as an online-enriched record because temporary works deformation is a high-consequence event where current site conditions and recent load history materially affect interpretation.

title: Confined-space gas detector alarm triggered during drainage inspection
observation: A gas detector alarm activated during drainage inspection, indicating an unsafe atmosphere in or near the confined space. The condition may involve oxygen deficiency, toxic gas, or flammable gas, and no further entry should continue until the atmosphere is proven safe.
recommended_action: Withdraw all personnel from the confined space area and prevent re-entry.; Test atmosphere from outside the space using calibrated equipment at multiple levels.; Implement forced ventilation only if approved and appropriate for the hazard identified.; Confirm rescue arrangements, permit status, and competent supervision before any re-entry.; Investigate source conditions and revise the confined-space entry controls accordingly.
escalation: Escalate immediately to confined-space rescue resources and site management if anyone is inside the space, if gas readings are unstable, or if there is any sign of distress.
image_caption: A worker is positioned near a manhole opening with a handheld gas detector displaying an alarm state.
root_cause_hypothesis: Likely accumulation of sewer gas, inadequate ventilation, residual biological decay, or disturbed sediment releasing hazardous vapors.
offline_cache_reason: Retain as an online-enriched record because gas alarm events benefit from current contextual cues, permit status, and sensor-driven interpretation in a hybrid CV-RAG workflow.

title: Mobile crane lift conducted near overhead service line
observation: A mobile crane is operating within close proximity to an overhead service line, creating a life-threatening electrocution and equipment contact hazard. The lift path appears to intersect or approach the line envelope, requiring immediate control.
recommended_action: Stop the lift and hold all crane movements until the hazard is verified and cleared.; Establish the exact location, type, and minimum approach distance of the overhead service line.; Reposition the crane, landing area, or load path to maintain compliant clearance.; Brief the lifting team and appoint a dedicated spotter for line awareness if operations resume.; Obtain utility or competent authority coordination where required before any further lifting.
escalation: Escalate immediately to lifting supervisor and site emergency response if contact occurs, if clearance cannot be confirmed, or if the line is energized and uncontrolled proximity persists.
image_caption: A mobile crane boom is extended near an overhead utility line, with the lifting area active below.
visual_clues: Extended crane boom near overhead line
root_cause_hypothesis: Likely poor lift planning, incorrect identification of service line location, inadequate exclusion distances, or failure to coordinate with utility controls.
offline_cache_reason: Retain as an online-enriched record because lift proximity hazards depend heavily on current site logistics and active utility coordination, making online updates especially useful.
